questions.scenario: A script runs multiple commands. Without error handling, a failure in step 2 silently allows step 3 to run on bad data.
questions.question: Write a two-command sequence where set -e causes the script to stop at the first failure.
questions.description: set -e (errexit) exits the script immediately when any command returns non-zero. Without it, scripts blunder through failures silently. Part of the mandatory header: set -euo pipefail. The e and u and pipefail work together — each catches a different class of failure.
use_cases: Prevent cascading failures in deploy scripts
Stop a backup script if pg_dump fails
Ensure setup steps all succeed before continuing
hints: set -e at the top, then run a failing command followed by echo
Run: bash -c 'set -e; ls /nonexistent; echo this_should_not_print' 2>/dev/null; echo "exit: $?"

questions.scenario: A script references $UNDEFINED_VAR by accident. Without set -u, this expands to empty string silently.
questions.question: Demonstrate set -u: show that referencing an unset variable causes an immediate error.
questions.description: set -u (nounset) treats references to unset variables as errors. Without it, $TYPO_VAR expands to empty string — the script continues with wrong data. With set -u, the script aborts at the point of the bad reference.
use_cases: Prevent silent failures from undefined config vars
Enforce that all required variables are set
hints: set -u then reference an undefined variable
The script should fail with an 'unbound variable' error

questions.scenario: A script creates a temp file. It must be cleaned up whether the script succeeds or fails.
questions.question: Set up a trap that removes /tmp/lab-trap.txt on EXIT, create the file, then verify the trap runs.
questions.description: trap 'command' SIGNAL runs the command when the signal occurs. EXIT fires on any exit (normal or error). ERR fires on command failure. INT fires on Ctrl+C. Traps are the bash equivalent of try/finally.
use_cases: Clean up temp files on exit
Log when a script terminates unexpectedly
hints: Create the file, then the trap cleans it on exit
Run: bash -c 'trap "rm -f /tmp/lab-trap.txt" EXIT; touch /tmp/lab-trap.txt' && test ! -f /tmp/lab-trap.txt && echo 'cleaned up'

validation.command: bash -c 'trap "rm -f /tmp/lab-trap.txt" EXIT; touch /tmp/lab-trap.txt' && test ! -f /tmp/lab-trap.txt && echo 'cleaned up'

questions.scenario: A script is failing at an unknown line. You need to see every command as it executes.
questions.question: Use set -x to trace execution of a three-command script and observe the output.
questions.description: set -x prints each command to stderr before executing it, prefixed with +. This is the bash debugger — you see exactly what's running and what values variables have at execution time. Disable with set +x.
use_cases: Debug scripts that produce wrong output
Trace variable values through a complex script
Understand what a third-party script is doing
hints: Run a few commands and see the + prefixed lines
Run: bash -c 'set -x; X=42; echo $X; Y=$((X*2)); echo $Y' 2>&1 | head -6

questions.scenario: A script fails at line N but you don't know which line that is. $LINENO tells you.
questions.question: Write a trap that prints the line number when an error occurs using $LINENO.
questions.description: $LINENO holds the current line number being executed. Combined with trap 'handler' ERR, you get the exact line of any failure. $BASH_SOURCE gives the filename. Together these form the minimal crash reporter for bash scripts.
use_cases: Identify exactly where a script fails in production
Add line numbers to error log entries
Debug scripts without adding echo statements everywhere
hints: Run: bash -c 'trap '"'"'echo ERROR at line $LINENO'"'"' ERR; ls /nonexistent' 2>/dev/null

validation.command: bash -c 'trap '"'"'echo ERROR at line $LINENO'"'"' ERR; ls /nonexistent' 2>/dev/null

questions.scenario: Before running a script, you must verify it passes static analysis. shellcheck catches bugs before they run.
questions.question: Run shellcheck on /home/student/scripts/broken.sh and observe what it reports.
questions.description: shellcheck performs static analysis on bash scripts. It catches unquoted variables, undefined variables, deprecated syntax, common mistakes, and style issues — before any code runs. Run it on every script before executing.
use_cases: Catch quoting bugs before they cause rm -rf disasters
Enforce consistent safe coding style in a team
